[{"role": "user", "metadata": null, "content": [{"text": "What problems does Barbara solve?", "type": "text"}], "options": null}, {"role": "assistant", "metadata": null, "content": [{"text": "I help organizations—and sometimes individuals—turn messy, hard-to-interpret data into systems that people can trust and actually use. That usually means building tools or pipelines that bridge noisy input (logs, sensor streams, text, event histories) with structured, interpretable outputs—things like knowledge graphs, retrieval layers, or dashboards built for human-in-the-loop decision making. 

The underlying problem is always about meaning extraction: making it possible for teams, users, or decision-makers to find what matters in a sea of information and act on it with confidence. Sometimes that looks like improving document or question-answering for a support use case (think building RAG systems and ground-truth evaluation frameworks), other times it means architecting a personal analytics dashboard that motivates real behavioral change (like a fitness tracker that actually helps you see what makes a difference). Across all of these, I bring two core elements: an understanding of how people process complex information, and the engineering chops to build production-worthy solutions.

That is different from just deploying AI tools as-is, or optimizing pipelines for scale only. My niche is connecting human and machine intelligence—making sure what I ship actually integrates with the way people think, search, and decide. If you want specific examples, I can dive into projects like the **Fitness Dashboard**, the **Beehive Tracker**, or my work building a retrieval and evaluation layer at **Inflective**.", "type": "text"}], "options": null}]

[{"role": "user", "metadata": null, "content": [{"text": "Walk me through a project", "type": "text"}], "options": null}, {"role": "assistant", "metadata": null, "content": [{"text": "Let’s take my **Self-Hosted Workout Intelligence** project as a concrete example. The problem came from something personal—like a lot of folks during the pandemic, I suddenly had a pile of messy fitness data spread across my Apple Watch, Strava, and old spreadsheets. The problem: none of these sources talked to each other, and the dashboards they offered didn’t help me see deeper patterns—or build actual new habits.

My insight: instead of forcing everything into one giant (and brittle) data table, I could treat each workout as an event with rich metadata and link them into a **knowledge graph**. That way, I could ask more interesting questions: not just “How many workouts did I do?”, but “What kind of diversity am I getting month-over-month?”, or “How do sleep and recovery patterns connect with workout quality over time?” The “aha” moment was applying both cognitive science (thinking about memory and attention) and practical AI engineering (event extraction, classification, entity linking).

I built a Python backend using **Neo4j** for the graph, added pipelines for syncing data from APIs and CSVs, wrote custom logic for workout classification (a small ML model), and designed a Streamlit-based dashboard where I could explore patterns interactively—drilling down from summary stats to individual workout histories, even surfacing streaks and behavioral cues. For me, visual literacy is key, so there’s a whole layer of diagrams and small multiples to make the logic and habits visible.

The system did more than track numbers—it helped me identify gaps, hold myself accountable, and actually tune my routines. Alongside this, the ontology and event graph pattern ended up influencing my later projects, like **Beehive Analytics** (for beekeeping data) and **Digital Memory Chest** (for personal photos with rich metadata).

If you want stories from another project—say, the **CareerCraft Job Assistant**, which tackled messy career data and job transitions—I’m happy to walk through that arc as well.", "type": "text"}], "options": null}]

[{"role": "user", "metadata": null, "content": [{"text": "How was this digital twin built?", "type": "text"}], "options": null}, {"role": "assistant", "metadata": null, "content": [{"text": "The Digital Twin is a portfolio piece that shows exactly how I approach knowledge engineering and applied AI. I built it as a retrieval-augmented generation (**RAG**) system, which means it doesn’t just run a generic chatbot — it retrieves actual facts from a curated knowledge base about me, my work, and my philosophy, then uses a language model to weave those facts into conversation.

The core technical choices:

- **Knowledge base design, not just storage:** I didn’t just dump documents or my resume into a vector store. I wrote the documents to maximize retrieval quality — breaking things into granular, question-sized nuggets, highlighting connections across projects, and shaping the context to support nuanced questions (“how does her career fit together?” not just “what university did she attend?”). This shifts the focus from “store everything” to “shape the input for the questions real users will ask.” That’s a directly transferable skill to building RAG for enterprise or healthcare, where quality-of-retrieval beats quantity-of-documents every time.

- **ChromaDB for embedding storage:** I use ChromaDB with OpenAI’s **text-embedding-3-small** to power fast, high-quality retrieval.

- **Language model orchestration:** Once the system pulls the top context pieces, the LLM (OpenAI) is prompted to generate answers that match my voice, thinking style, and knowledge boundaries. This means it won’t hallucinate papers I haven’t published or technologies I haven’t used — it’s grounded and honest about what’s in the knowledge base.

- **Deployment and monitoring:** The system runs on AWS EC2 with SSL, a custom domain, and basic error/latency monitoring — proving I can take a GenAI product all the way to reliable user access.

What’s different about this from any portfolio site or typical chatbot is what it demonstrates: I don’t see knowledge engineering as just “collecting information.” It’s about designing for **real retrieval under noise and ambiguity**, just like how humans use memory and attention in the wild. The Digital Twin is as much a methodology proof-point as it is a technical demo.

Related: the Twin connects to projects like **Resume Explorer** (which builds career knowledge graphs) and **Weaving Memories** (which extracts legacy knowledge from biographical text). Each one reflects this same philosophy of making meaning out of messy data, for a real user need.", "type": "text"}], "options": null}]

[{"role": "user", "metadata": null, "content": [{"text": "What does 'making meaning from messy data' actually mean?", "type": "text"}], "options": null}, {"role": "assistant", "metadata": null, "content": [{"text": "Making meaning from messy data isn’t about cleaning up a spreadsheet until it’s tidy and fits into a model. It’s about looking at all the ambiguity, the gaps, and the inconsistencies, and recognizing that the things which don’t fit are often what actually matter. The “mess” exists because the data was generated by humans, for human purposes, in rich, lived contexts.

Here’s what I do: I take something chaotic—a pile of tagged resumes, a decade of handwritten workout logs, hive inspection photos, or the scattered artifacts from a parent’s life—and I build a structure that lets the important patterns reveal themselves. That structure isn’t arbitrary. Sometimes it’s a graph connecting skills, sometimes it’s a timeline, sometimes it’s an ontology, but it always makes relationships and trends visible in a way that you can reason about.

The end goal isn’t to spit out one key metric. It’s to create a representation—maybe a graph, maybe a report, maybe a dashboard—where you (or a user, or an automated agent) can actually see how things evolve, cluster, or connect. For example: a resume becomes a skill map that shows how someone’s career really unfolded, or a set of workout logs turns into a visualization of training rhythms across seasons. The meaning was there in the mess; my job is to surface it so people can use it.", "type": "text"}], "options": null}]
